region_of_origin: India (Punjab / North India); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Baingan Bharta as a prepared dish; 'eggplant food product' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible anchor for the primary ingredient.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Aubergine/eggplant, cooked) is the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for the spiced, roasted-and-mashed preparation style of Baingan Bharta.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Baingan Bharta or roasted eggplant dish as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Baigan Bharta is approximately 300 g, typically served as a side dish alongside roti, naan, or steamed rice in an Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked brinjal/eggplant dish with oil and spices, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt' (FDC ID 169228) and adjusted for oil addition (~7g fat/100g cooked dish). The input record values (energy 106.67 kcal, protein 2.17 g, fat 7.33 g, carbohydrate 8.33 g) are consistent with this composition and are retained. Sodium 150 mg/100g reflects moderate salt addition during cooking. Cholesterol is 0 mg as the dish is entirely plant-based.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Baingan Bharta. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for eggplant/aubergine (raw and cooked), which is consistently classified as a low-GI vegetable (GI < 20). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 24.99) / 100 ≈ 3.7, rounded to 4.0. The low carbohydrate content (8.33 g/100g × 300g serving = ~25g) and high fibre (2.67 g/100g) further support a low GL classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (150 mg/100g) with beneficial potassium from eggplant, tomato, and onion
hypertension.guidance: Baigan Bharta is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared with minimal added salt; the natural potassium content from eggplant (~230 mg/100g raw), tomato, and onion supports a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio, and patients should avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), low carbohydrate (8.33 g/100g), and good dietary fibre (2.67 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baigan Bharta is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI (~15), modest carbohydrate load, and meaningful fibre content that slows glucose absorption; it should be paired with wholegrain bread or brown rice rather than refined carbohydrates, and cooking oil quantity should be controlled to limit caloric density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (1.17 g/100g), and plant-based fibre from eggplant
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baigan Bharta poses a low cardiovascular risk as it contains no cholesterol, low saturated fat, and beneficial dietary fibre; however, the total fat content (7.33 g/100g) reflects added cooking oil, so individuals with CVD should request preparation with minimal oil and avoid trans-fat-containing cooking fats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, tomato, green chilli) contain solanine, which may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive RA individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should avoid or limit Baigan Bharta due to its primary ingredient (eggplant) and secondary nightshades (tomato, green chilli); the anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger in the masala are beneficial and may be retained if nightshade vegetables are substituted.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baigan Bharta (also spelled Baingan Bharta; Hindi: बैंगन भर्ता) is a classic North Indian vegetarian dish in which whole eggplant (brinjal/aubergine) is roasted directly over an open flame or in an oven until the skin chars and the flesh becomes soft and smoky. The roasted eggplant is then peeled, mashed, and stir-fried with sautéed onions, tomatoes, green chillies, garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander powder, and garam masala. The dish is finished with fresh coriander leaves. The result is a smoky, earthy, mildly spiced mash with a soft, creamy texture. 'Pind Se' (Punjabi: ਪਿੰਡ ਤੋਂ) translates roughly as 'from the village' or 'village-style', indicating a rustic, traditional folk preparation. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides approximately 107 kcal, 2.2g protein, 7.3g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), 8.3g carbohydrate, 2.7g dietary fibre, and 150mg sodium. Cholesterol is zero as the dish is entirely plant-based. The glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The high fibre and potassium content from eggplant, tomato, and onion support cardiovascular and blood pressure health. Eggplant is a nightshade vegetable and may be a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis sensitive to solanine. The dish is naturally halal, vegan, vegetarian, and gluten-free. It is commonly served as a side dish with roti, naan, paratha, or steamed rice in Indian cuisine, and is popular across India, Pakistan, and the South Asian diaspora in Singapore and Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked eggplant dish with oil and spices, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central. The input values are internally consistent and plausible for this preparation style. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry for 'Baingan Bharta' as a named dish was found; values represent an adapted estimate from the closest approved-source equivalents. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for eggplant.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Baingan Bharta is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a named composite dish; only total fat and saturated fat are reported. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: while eggplant contains approximately 230 mg potassium/100g raw, the potassium content of the cooked composite dish (with oil, tomato, onion, spices) is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA for this preparation; a reliable composite figure is not available from approved sources. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a named Baingan Bharta entry. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — the dish is entirely plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed by USDA FoodData Central for all constituent vegetables and plant oils. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.03: trace level from cooking oil; below meaningful detection threshold for a plant-based dish prepared with standard vegetable oil. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database classification of eggplant/aubergine as a low-GI vegetable (GI consistently below 20). 7. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not on the approved source list; ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central were used instead. 8. Ethnic tags 'Naan' and 'Biryani' from the input were not carried forward as they are dish names, not ethnic identifiers. 9. country_of_origin in the input was listed as 'Singapore'; this has been corrected to reflect the dish's true origin in North India (Punjab), with Singapore noted as the serving context.
